title: What clawmark Does
description: Start with the README and the agent-context files to understand the problem clawmark solves. The project answers one focused question: which of two CLAUDE.md files makes Claude perform better on real software-engineering bugs? v1 compares exactly two local variant files (Variant A and Variant B) against a fixed set of five SWE-bench Lite tasks from the astropy repository, runs Claude locally, evaluates the generated patches with the official SWE-bench harness in Docker, and prints a simple A/B summary. AGENTS.md captures all guiding constraints for the project—minimal dependencies, no scope creep, ship the smoke runner first—and is the canonical source of truth for contributors and AI agents alike.

title: Entry Point: main.rs
description: main.rs is the executable entry point. It parses the top-level CLI struct with Clap, then dispatches to one of three subcommand handlers: doctor::run_doctor(), runner::run_ab(), or report::compute_report(). The run subcommand is the only one that builds a Tokio multi-thread runtime—needed for async parallel Claude invocations—while doctor and report are fully synchronous. All three return an integer exit code; main.rs calls std::process::exit with it so the shell sees success or failure.

title: CLI Argument Parsing & Validation: cli.rs
description: cli.rs defines the three Clap structs—Cli, RunArgs, and ReportArgs—and the Commands enum that main.rs matches on. The most important concept here is the two-phase design: raw args (RunArgs) are parsed by Clap and then validated by validate_with_cwd() to produce a ValidatedRunArgs that guarantees invariants the rest of the code depends on. Validation checks that --a and --b resolve to different files inside the current working directory, that --timeout-secs is in range, that the output directory does not already exist (runs are never resumed or overwritten), and that --parallel is at least 1. For report, validate() confirms that harness/a.json and harness/b.json already exist before any I/O is attempted.

title: Prerequisite Checker: doctor.rs
description: doctor.rs implements the doctor subcommand, which probes the local environment before a user attempts a full run. It checks that docker, the claude CLI, python3 (with the swebench package importable), and git are all present and functional. Each check is a simple subprocess invocation—no shell strings, always argv arrays. Failures are printed clearly with actionable messages. Running clawmark doctor before clawmark run is the recommended first step documented in the README, because the SWE-bench harness requires Docker and the claude CLI requires local authentication.

title: The Benchmark Dataset: swebench.rs & the Bundled JSONL
description: swebench.rs defines the two core data types—TaskInstance (one SWE-bench problem: repo, base commit, and problem statement) and Prediction (a generated patch)—and owns the five fixed smoke instance IDs in the SMOKE_INSTANCE_IDS constant. The entire bundled dataset is embedded at compile time via include_str!, so no external file access is required at runtime. load_bundled_smoke_set() parses the embedded JSONL string line by line into Vec<TaskInstance>. The data file itself, data/swebench_lite_v1_subset.jsonl, contains exactly the five astropy tasks that v1 always runs in fixed order. The module also provides validate_repo_slug and validate_base_commit to guard against path-traversal or injection before any git URL is constructed.

title: Workspace Sandbox: sandbox.rs
description: sandbox.rs is responsible for the per-task, per-variant isolated workspace. create() clones the task's GitHub repository at the exact base commit into a tempfile::TempDir, ensuring the workspace is automatically cleaned up when the Workspace struct is dropped. inject_claude_md() then writes the chosen variant's contents as CLAUDE.md at the repo root—this is the mechanism by which the A/B test works, since Claude auto-discovers CLAUDE.md for instructions. After Claude exits, collect_patch() runs git diff HEAD to capture any changes Claude made; an empty diff is a valid result (the task was not resolved). All git invocations use argv arrays with no shell interpolation.

title: Shared Types & Serialization: results.rs
description: results.rs is the I/O and type backbone shared between runner.rs and report.rs. It defines HarnessResult (the structure the SWE-bench harness writes, keyed on resolved_ids), SwebenchPrediction (the format the harness expects as input), and named path constants for every output file (run_records.jsonl, predictions/a.jsonl, harness/a.json, report.json, etc.). Key functions: variant_hash() computes a SHA-256 fingerprint of the variant file contents so each RunRecord is traceable to an exact variant; append_run_record() appends JSONL records atomically; write_predictions_jsonl() uses a NamedTempFile then persists to avoid partial writes; load_harness_results() reads and validates the narrow resolved_ids-only schema from harness output.

title: Core A/B Runner: runner.rs
description: runner.rs orchestrates the entire benchmark run. run_ab() is the async top-level: it loads the five tasks, reads and hashes both variant files, creates the output directory tree, then calls run_variant() for A then B serially. run_variant() fans out over tasks using a Tokio semaphore to bound concurrency (--parallel controls the semaphore size), spawning each task as a spawn_blocking call so the blocking git/claude subprocesses don't block the async runtime. run_single() is the per-task unit: sandbox.create → sandbox.inject_claude_md → invoke_claude → sandbox.collect_patch → produce RunRecord. invoke_claude() builds the exact claude argv (-p --output-format json --dangerously-skip-permissions --model <model> --add-dir <workspace> -- <problem_statement>), polls the child process with a 100ms spin loop, and distinguishes auth failures (which abort the whole run) from per-task failures (which are stored on the record). After both variants finish, invoke_harness() runs the SWE-bench Python harness in Docker for each, then the report is computed and printed inline.

title: A/B Report Generation: report.rs
description: report.rs reads harness/a.json and harness/b.json, treats their resolved_ids arrays as the sole source of truth, and computes per-task outcomes. For each of the five smoke tasks it checks whether A resolved it, B resolved it, both, or neither, yielding four aggregate counters: a_wins, b_wins, ties_both_resolved, ties_both_failed. render_terminal_table() prints the human-readable summary. write_report_json() persists the Report struct to report.json via write_atomic_json. The report subcommand lets you re-render this table from a completed output directory without re-running anything; the run subcommand also calls these functions inline after the harness finishes.

title: Variant Files: What You're Actually Comparing
description: The whole point of clawmark is to compare two CLAUDE.md files. variants/a.md and variants/b.md are the example variant files checked into the repo for local testing. You pass them as --a variants/a.md --b variants/b.md to clawmark run. Each variant is written as CLAUDE.md at the root of every cloned task workspace before Claude is invoked, so Claude reads that file as its system prompt/instructions for the task. The content difference between the two files is what drives the A/B comparison—clawmark measures whether those instructions lead to more or fewer resolved SWE-bench tasks.

title: Build Configuration & Dependencies: Cargo.toml
description: Cargo.toml declares the crate as a 2021-edition binary named clawmark, enforces unsafe_code = "forbid" via [lints.rust], and lists the minimal set of dependencies: clap (CLI parsing), serde + serde_json (serialization), sha2 + hex (variant fingerprinting), tempfile (sandbox temp directories), tokio (async runtime for parallel invocations), and tempfile. The project intentionally avoids anyhow, thiserror, tokio async I/O, and any model-output parsing. Cargo.lock pins exact versions for reproducible builds.

title: CI Pipeline & End-to-End Smoke Test
description: The GitHub Actions workflow in .github/workflows/ci.yml runs cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy, and cargo test on every push and pull request, keeping the codebase formatted and lint-clean without requiring Docker. The end-to-end integration test lives in scripts/e2e_smoke.sh—a Bash script that wires together all three subcommands (doctor, run, report) against a real Docker environment to verify the full pipeline works. The planning documents under .planning/ (PROJECT.md, REQUIREMENTS.md, ROADMAP.md, STATE.md) capture the GSD workflow state and phase history for the maintainer.
